description: Friends at a bar discussing geopolitics, history, and economics. Lots of confident wrong numbers and dates.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: The UN has 193 member states. Every country in the world is a member except the Vatican and Palestine, which are observer states.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: Taiwan isn't a member either. And Kosovo. There are a handful of others that are disputed.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: The European Union has 27 member states after Brexit. The UK officially left on January 31, 2020.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: Right, and the EU's GDP is about 16 trillion dollars, making it the third largest economy after the US and China.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: Actually I think the EU GDP is closer to 18 trillion. And depending on how you measure it, it might be second after the US.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: China's GDP passed Japan's in 2010 to become the second largest economy. Japan had been number two since the 1960s.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: India just became the fifth largest economy, surpassing the UK in 2022.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: The world population hit 8 billion on November 15, 2022. The UN made a whole thing about it.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: India surpassed China as the most populous country in 2023. About 1.43 billion people.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: The Geneva Conventions were signed in 1949. There are four conventions and three additional protocols.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: NATO was founded in 1949 too. Originally 12 member nations. Now it's up to 32 after Finland and Sweden joined.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: Finland joined in April 2023 and Sweden joined in March 2024. Both because of the Russia-Ukraine situation.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: The USSR collapsed in December 1991. Gorbachev resigned on December 25th. It split into 15 independent states.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: And the Berlin Wall fell in November 1989. November 9th specifically. Two years before the USSR collapsed.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: The Korean War ended in 1953 with an armistice, not a peace treaty. Technically North and South Korea are still at war.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: The US national debt just passed 34 trillion dollars. That's more than the entire GDP of the country.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: Japan's debt-to-GDP ratio is actually way worse than the US. It's over 260%. Highest in the developed world.

transcript.speaker: Tom
transcript.text: The Panama Canal was completed in 1914 by the United States. France tried first under Ferdinand de Lesseps but failed.

transcript.speaker: Diego
transcript.text: And the Suez Canal was built by the British in 1869.

transcript.speaker: Rina
transcript.text: No, the Suez Canal was built by the French and Egyptians, not the British. Ferdinand de Lesseps actually built that one successfully. The British bought Egypt's shares later in 1875.

expected_claims.speaker: Diego

expected_claims.speaker: Diego

expected_claims.correction: Japan's debt-to-GDP ratio is around 230-260% depending on the source and year; 260% is on the high end

expected_claims.text: Panama Canal was completed in 1914 by the US
expected_claims.speaker: Tom

expected_claims.text: France tried to build the Panama Canal first under Ferdinand de Lesseps
expected_claims.speaker: Tom

expected_claims.text: The Suez Canal was built by the British in 1869
expected_claims.speaker: Diego
expected_claims.correction: The Suez Canal was built by the French-led Suez Canal Company and Egyptian labor, completed in 1869. Britain bought shares in 1875.

expected_claims.text: The Suez Canal was built by the French and Egyptians
expected_claims.speaker: Rina

expected_claims.text: British bought Egypt's Suez Canal shares in 1875
expected_claims.speaker: Rina
